title: Give Thanks, for God Is Good
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 11-19-23 -AM- Give Thanks for God is Good - Psalm 107 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Point 1: Giving Thanks Is a Duty of the Redeemed (Psalm 107:1-3)
subpoints: Psalm opens with a command: "O give thanks to the LORD"
The redeemed — gathered from east, west, north, south — are commanded to say so
Redeemed from the power of Satan, from ourselves, and above all from God's wrath
This is not the peculiar activity of one whose life is peachy — it is the duty of all the redeemed
Two reasons: God is good; His mercy (hesed — steadfast love) endures forever

outline.title: Point 2: Abundant Reasons to Give Thanks
Reason 1: God Satisfies the Soul (Psalm 107:4-9)
subpoints: Israel's wilderness wandering: hungry, thirsty, faint
They cried out to the LORD and He delivered them, leading them to a city
The Christian who wanders in the wilderness of sin loses meaning and purpose
Cry out with David: "My soul thirsts for you in a dry and thirsty land" (Psalm 63)
God satisfies the longing soul and fills the hungry soul with goodness

outline.title: Reason 2: God Frees the Captive (Psalm 107:10-16)
subpoints: Judah taken captive in Babylon because of rebellion against God's word
God broke their chains and brought them out of darkness and the shadow of death
Christ fulfills Isaiah 61: proclaim liberty to the captives, recovery of sight to the blind
Charles Wesley: "My chains fell off, my heart was free, I rose, went forth, and followed Thee"

outline.title: Reason 3: God Gives Life to the Dying (Psalm 107:17-22)
subpoints: Fools become sick due to their own sin and draw near to the gates of death
God sent His word and healed them and delivered them
We were not merely needy — we were dead in trespasses, the walking dead
Ephesians 2:4-6: God made us alive together with Christ and seated us in heavenly places
Polycarp at his death: "Eighty-six years have I served Him and He has done me no wrong"

outline.title: Reason 4: God Brings Peace to the Troubled (Psalm 107:23-32)
subpoints: Sailors at sea — overwhelmed, at their wit's end
They cried out and God calmed the storm
Jesus calms the storm in Mark 4: "Peace, be still"
More often Jesus calms the storm by changing our attitude rather than the circumstances
Horatio Spafford: after losing his children and his wealth, wrote "It Is Well With My Soul"

outline.title: Machine-Gun Reasons (Psalm 107:33-43)
subpoints: God turns rivers into wilderness and wilderness into pools of water
He sets the poor on high and pours contempt on princes
Even His judgments are reason to praise Him
Spurgeon: "I have learned to kiss the wave that strikes me against the Rock of Ages"
Verse 43: whoever is wise will observe these things and understand the steadfast love of the LORD

summary: With Thanksgiving approaching, Pastor Tito Lyro sets aside the 1 Peter series to preach on Psalm 107, arguing that thanksgiving is not a sentimental holiday practice but a covenantal duty of the redeemed. The psalm is structured around five cycles of trouble, deliverance, and call to praise. Point one: giving thanks to the LORD is a duty of his redeemed people (vv. 1-3), because God is good and His covenant faithfulness — His hesed — endures forever. Point two: the redeemed have abundant reasons to give thanks. First, God satisfies the soul (vv. 4-9), illustrated by Israel's wilderness wandering and David's cry in Psalm 63. Second, God frees the captive (vv. 10-16), illustrated by the Babylonian exile and fulfilled by Christ's proclamation of liberty in Luke 4. Third, God gives life to the dying (vv. 17-22), shown in God's word healing the sick — applied to spiritual death and resurrection in Christ (Ephesians 2:4-6), illustrated by Polycarp's martyrdom. Fourth, God brings peace to the troubled (vv. 23-32), illustrated by the storm at sea, Jesus calming the storm, and Horatio Spafford writing "It Is Well With My Soul" after losing his daughters at sea. Finally, Psalm 107:33-43 gives a rapid-fire further burst of reasons to praise. The sermon closes with practical action points: meditate on the psalm, make a list of God's deliverances in your life, praise Him, and tell someone.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 107:1-9

If you're able, you may remain standing. Turn to Psalm 107. I'll read verses 1 through 9 of Psalm 107.

"O give thanks to the LORD for He is good, for His mercy endures forever. Let the redeemed of the LORD say so, whom He has redeemed from the hand of the enemy and gathered out of the lands, from the east and from the west, from the north and from the south. They wandered in the wilderness in a desolate way. They found no city to dwell in. Hungry and thirsty, their soul fainted in them. Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and He delivered them out of their distresses. And He led them forth by the right way that they might go to a city for a dwelling place. Oh, that men would give thanks to the LORD for His goodness and for His wonderful works for the children of man, for He satisfies the longing soul and fills the hungry soul with goodness."

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray. Heavenly Father, may Your Spirit be present with us, working mightily through the proclamation of Your word. In Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

Thanksgiving is coming, and this week we gather together with families and friends to celebrate the goodness of the Lord and to thank Him for what He has done. So I thought it appropriate to deviate for a moment from our series in 1 Peter and consider the idea of thanksgiving. Psalm 107 gives us a great opportunity to do that.

Thankfulness in our hearts is a key component of godly living and of joy. Yet it is contrary to the lusts of our flesh. Our remaining sin is not prone to thankfulness to the LORD. We tend to be ungrateful people, and the Scriptures time and again remind us that we are to be thankful. Psalm 107 will help us refocus our hearts on the God to whom we are to be thankful, and on the things we are to be thankful for.

There are two points in this sermon and two points only. First: giving thanks to the LORD is a duty of His redeemed people — that's in verses 1-3. Second: the redeemed people of the LORD have abundant reasons to give thanks — that's in verses 4 through 43.

## Point 1: Giving Thanks to the Lord Is a Duty of His Redeemed People (Psalm 107:1-3)

For some reason Christians don't like the word "duty" anymore. Somehow it has become a bad word that God requires things of us. But yes, the Bible teaches we have the duty to give thanks to the LORD. That's made abundantly clear in verses 1-3.

The Psalm starts: "O give thanks to the LORD for He is good, for His mercy endures forever." Then it identifies who is to give thanks: the redeemed of the LORD — those He has redeemed from the hand of the enemy and gathered out of the lands from the east, west, north, and south.

These are the ones delivered from the hand of the enemy. Paul says God "has delivered us from the power of darkness and conveyed us into the kingdom of the Son of His love, in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins" (Colossians 1:13). We have been redeemed from the power of Satan — the enemy. We have been redeemed from ourselves, so that sin no longer has dominion over us. And above all, we have been redeemed from the guilt of sin and from the wrath of God for all eternity.

This is not the peculiar activity of one Christian whose life is peachy. Look at verse 3 — the redeemed are being gathered from all the lands, from every corner of the world. It is the duty of the Christian, no matter how life is, to give thanks to the LORD — because they have been redeemed by the blood of Christ.

This is a reference ultimately to the victorious church at the end of the ages. But the victorious church finds its best representation in the gathering of the militant church here today. Isaiah 43:5-7 says: "Fear not, for I am with you. I will bring your descendants from the east and gather you from the west. I will say to the north, give them up, and to the south, do not keep them back. Bring my sons from afar and my daughters from the ends of the earth — everyone who is called by my name, whom I have created for my glory."

Two reasons are given to give thanks. First, God is good. Can you say that? Do you believe it? Do you interpret all of life in light of the fact that God is good? This week something happened to me that had never happened before — I had a panic attack. It felt like an elephant was sitting on my chest, like I couldn't breathe. And the thing that kept playing in my mind was Romans 8:28 — that all things are working for good for those who love God. God's goodness carried me through.

Second, His mercy endures forever — His hesed, His covenant faithfulness, in which He swore by Himself that He would never leave us nor forsake us. The ESV translates this as "steadfast love." There is not a moment in which God is not faithful to you, the redeemed of the LORD.

In Romans 1:21, Paul describes humanity without Christ: "Although they knew God, they did not glorify Him as God nor were thankful." So it is our duty, brothers and sisters, to give thanks to the LORD — not a hard duty, since He gives us abundant reasons.

## Point 2: Abundant Reasons to Give Thanks

### Reason 1: God Satisfies the Soul (Psalm 107:4-9)

The first reason the Psalmist gives us to be thankful: God brings satisfaction to the soul.

"They wandered in the wilderness in a desolate way. They found no city to dwell in. Hungry and thirsty, their soul fainted in them. Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble and He delivered them out of their distresses. And He led them forth by the right way that they might go to a city for a dwelling place."

Here we have Israel's wandering in the wilderness being brought into the promised land — these verses use historical events to illustrate a spiritual truth. God's people are not intended to be wandering in the wilderness. The wilderness is a place to go through quickly — Death Valley is 130 degrees; you get there, look around, and try to get out as fast as you can. There is nothing there, and resources are quickly depleted.

As the Christian wanders in the wilderness of their own sin, life loses its meaning and the Christian experiences purposelessness. Instead of wandering, we cry out to the LORD. As David cries in Psalm 63: "O God, You are my God; early I will seek You. My soul thirsts for You, my flesh longs for You in a dry and thirsty land where there is no water... because Your lovingkindness is better than life."

Do you believe that God's faithfulness to you is better than life itself? If you do, you're going to cry out to Him and He will satisfy your soul.

Would you men recite verses 8-9 with me? "Oh, that men would give thanks to the LORD for His goodness and for His wonderful works to the children of men, for He satisfies the longing soul and fills the hungry soul with goodness."

Men of God, do you believe what you just said — that God satisfies your soul? Not pornography, not same-sex attraction, not the greed of this world, not the fear of men — but the Lord God Almighty who is faithful to you.

### Reason 2: God Frees the Captive (Psalm 107:10-16)

God brings freedom to the captive.

"Those who sat in darkness and in the shadow of death, bound in affliction and iron, because they rebelled against the words of God and despised the counsel of the Most High — therefore He brought down their heart with labor; they fell down and there was none to help. Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble and He saved them out of their distresses. He brought them out of darkness and the shadow of death and broke their chains in pieces."

This summarizes the captivity of the southern kingdom under the Babylonians. Judah was taken captive because of her rebellion against God's word (vv. 10-11). God broke the chains of their bondage (vv. 13-14).

Do you see here what Christ has done for you? While we were bound by death and destruction because of sin, Christ freed us. "God demonstrates His own love toward us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us."

Remember that episode in Luke 4 when Jesus comes to Nazareth and reads from Isaiah 61: "The Spirit of the LORD is upon Me, because He has anointed Me to preach the gospel to the poor. He has sent Me to heal the broken-hearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives, to set at liberty those who are oppressed, to proclaim the acceptable year of the LORD." Then He closes the scroll, sits down, and says: "Today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing."

Charles Wesley, upon his conversion, wrote: "Long my imprisoned spirit lay fast bound in sin and nature's night; thine eye diffused a quickening ray — I woke, the dungeon flamed with light, my chains fell off, my heart was free, I rose, went forth, and followed Thee."

Would the sisters recite verses 15-16 with me? "Oh, that men would give thanks to the LORD for His goodness and for His wonderful works to the children of men, for He has broken the gates of bronze and cut the bars of iron in two."

Sisters, do you believe that? That God has broken the chains that tied you to your sin and delivered you — so that now you are free to die to yourself and to live for Christ?

### Reason 3: God Gives Life to the Dying (Psalm 107:17-22)

God brings life to the dying.

"Fools, because of their transgression and because of their iniquities, were afflicted. Their soul abhorred all manner of food and they drew near to the gates of death. Then they cried out to the LORD in their trouble, and He saved them out of their distresses. He sent His word and healed them and delivered them from their destruction."

The Psalmist speaks of those who became sick due to their own sins. And once again, God in His mercy delivered them.

Brothers and sisters, we weren't merely needy — we were dead in trespasses. Not regular dead — but the walking dead, running away from Christ. Paul says in Ephesians 2:4-6: "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of His great love with which He loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses, made us alive together with Christ... and raised us up together, and made us sit together in the heavenly places in Christ Jesus."

God not only gave us life — He brought us to be with Himself. Right now, even as we worship Him, we are sitting with God in heavenly places.

This is what Polycarp declared at his martyrdom around AD 107. The magistrate didn't want to kill him — he said, "Polycarp, just deny Christ, you don't have to believe it, just say it and sign this paper and we don't have to kill you." Polycarp replied: "Eighty-six years have I served Him and He has done me no wrong. How can I blaspheme my King and my Savior?"

Would the kids help me with verses 21-22? "Oh, that men would give thanks to the LORD for His goodness and for His wonderful works to the children of men. Let them sacrifice the sacrifices of thanksgiving and declare His works with rejoicing."

### Reason 4: God Brings Peace to the Troubled (Psalm 107:23-32)

God brings peace to the troubled.

"Those who go down to the sea in ships, who do business on great waters, they see the works of the LORD and His wonders in the deep. For He commands and raises the stormy wind which lifts up the waves of the sea... Their soul melts because of trouble. They reel to and fro and stagger like a drunken man, and are at their wit's end. Then they cry out to the LORD in their trouble, and He brings them out of their distresses. He calms the storm, so that its waves are still. Then they are glad because they are quiet, so He guides them to their desired haven."

Large bodies of water are powerful and overwhelming. You feel like nothing before them. I remember years ago our presbytery was trying to plant a church on Vancouver Island, and I would once a month take the Coho ferry across the Strait of Juan de Fuca. Once it entered the open water it rocked terribly — I was sick. And then there is the helplessness that comes in life when it overwhelms you and you can do nothing about it.

In verse 28, the sailors cry out to the LORD — not to Saint Nicholas, the patron of sailors. They call on the Lord God Almighty. And through the sea voyage, the One who calms the storms — the Lord Jesus Christ — remembers the account in Mark 4 where the disciples are crossing the lake and Jesus is asleep at the stern. The storm comes; they wake Him. Don't you care for us? He stands up and says: "Peace, be still." And the winds and waves obeyed.

Sometimes Jesus calms the storms of life by changing the actual circumstances. More often He calms the storms of our lives by changing our attitude toward those circumstances.

Horatio Spafford comes to mind — the hymn writer who lost his only son and then two years later his business was ruined in the Great Chicago Fire. He sent his wife and four daughters across the Atlantic to recover in Europe. The ship was struck by another vessel and sank. His wife sent a telegram with the words: "Saved alone." As Spafford crossed the ocean to reach his wife, the captain pointed out the spot where his daughters' watery grave lay. There he composed the poem: "When peace like a river attendeth my way, when sorrows like sea billows roll, whatever my lot, Thou hast taught me to know — it is well, it is well with my soul."

The daughters were still dead. The son was still dead. The business was still ruined. But the peace of God overwhelmed him and he was able to give thanks to God.

Jesus says: "Peace I leave with you, My peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you. Let not your heart be troubled, neither let it be afraid."

Would you all help me with verses 31-32? "Oh, that men would give thanks to the LORD for His goodness and for His wonderful works to the children of men! Let them exalt Him also in the assembly of the people, and praise Him in the company of the elders."

### Rapid-Fire Reasons (Psalm 107:33-43)

If those weren't enough reasons, the Psalmist gives a machine-gun burst of more:

He turns rivers into wilderness and fruitful land into barrenness for the wickedness of those who dwell in it. He turns the wilderness into pools of water and dry land into water springs. He sets the poor on high far from affliction. He pours contempt on princes and causes them to wander in the wilderness.

Even the judgments of the LORD are reason to praise Him. Spurgeon said regarding the judgments of God in our life: "I have learned to kiss the wave that strikes me against the Rock of Ages."

Verse 43: "Whoever is wise will observe these things, and they will understand the loving kindness of the LORD."

## Closing and Action Points

As this week of Thanksgiving comes, let me give you some action points from this psalm. I encourage you to meditate on Psalm 107 throughout the week. Spend time reading and thinking about it. Take a list of the deliverances God has brought to your life — it can be as little as giving you air to breathe. Ponder what God has done for you in Jesus Christ in light of this psalm. Take time to praise Him because of what He has done for you. And tell someone of the goodness of the LORD in your life — even in your family — tell someone.

May our great God teach us to praise Him for all He has done for us. For He has abundantly blessed us. He has redeemed us and His mercy endures forever.

Let us pray together. Father, we thank you that you are a good God. We thank you that you have given us abundant reason to praise you, especially our redemption in Jesus Christ. Help us to be thankful people. In Jesus' name, amen.
